text: Laparoscopic resection of a cervical diverticulum. Our objective is to review the clinical presentation and surgical management of a case of a cervical diverticulum. Our patient is a forty-one year old G-II P-II who presented with a seven month history of intermenstrual bleeding, bloating, and cramping. A transvaginal sonogram was obtained indicating an anteverted uterus and an eight centimeter right adnexal mass. The mass can be seen in this transvaginal image of the right adnexa adjacent to the uterus. An MRI of the pelvis was next performed showing a uterus with normal dimensions as well as a thick-walled tubular structure in the right adnexa measuring eight centimeters in length communicating with the lower uterine segment or upper cervix. The radiologist recommended ruling out a traumatic perforation. This is an MRI image of the pelvis showing enhanced uptake of dye in the area of the adnexal mass.

In the operating room, the surgeon identifies the pelvic mass located caudal to the uterine body, projecting towards the right pararectal area. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is dissected down to the bladder, followed by dissection of the posterior leaf towards the pelvic sidewall. The round ligament is transected, enhancing the view of the pelvic mass. Extensive retroperitoneal dissection is performed at the pelvic brim, extending towards the back of the bladder, with careful identification of the ureter.

The surgeon mobilizes the pelvic mass from its stalk, creating a bladder flap and pushing it down off the cervix and upper vagina. The mass is gradually moved to the right pararectal area, with the right ureter identified multiple times during dissection. The mass reveals a cavity forming a fistula with the right lower cervix, and the pedicle of this connection is transected before the specimen is removed.

The surgeon performs copious irrigation followed by chromoperturbation, revealing dye flow through a defect. The defect is repaired using barbed wire suture. Subsequently, the anterior and posterior leafs of the broad ligament are re-approximated to the round ligament using two ovicryl sutures.
The specimen is placed into a retrieval bag and extracted through a laparoscopic port. A hysteroscopy is performed, revealing an intact uterine cavity.

Pathology report showed a nine by seven point five centimeter aggregate of tan nodular tissue fragments without any areas of necrosis or calcification. Fragments of fibromuscular tissue were identified, surfaced by endocervical epithelium and focally with the lower uterine segment consistent with a cervical diverticulum. At the patient's three month post-op visit, she had no complaints and no further episodes of intermenstrual bleeding. Thank you.
